title: Hibernate Readiness Knowledge Base
summary: Guidance for using EMP Hibernate readiness evidence during Hibernate 5.x to 6.x modernization planning.
guide: Start with applicability. The Hibernate readiness pack is useful only when Hibernate ORM usage is detected from dependencies, imports, or mapping files.
Separate migration tracks. Jakarta namespace work, Hibernate API compatibility, custom type migration, and validation evidence should be planned as related but distinct workstreams.
Treat the static report as triage evidence. It identifies risk areas and repeatable discussion points before a consultant promises upgrade execution.

risks.name: Legacy Criteria API
risks.whyItMatters: Legacy org.hibernate.Criteria usage does not map cleanly to modern Hibernate migration paths.
risks.recommendation: Replace or isolate legacy Criteria usage before relying on automated upgrade validation.

risks.name: Direct Session API usage
risks.whyItMatters: Direct Session and SessionFactory usage often hides transaction, flush, and lifecycle assumptions.
risks.recommendation: Review Session-specific code paths and validate them with compile and test evidence.

risks.name: Custom type integrations
risks.whyItMatters: UserType, BasicType, and CompositeUserType integrations are sensitive to Hibernate type-system changes.
risks.recommendation: Inventory custom types and test them against the target Hibernate version before broad migration work.

risks.name: Hibernate XML mappings
risks.whyItMatters: hbm.xml mappings are easy to miss in source-only reviews and need explicit validation.
risks.recommendation: Keep XML mapping files in scope for upgrade review and test coverage.

risks.name: javax and Jakarta overlap
risks.whyItMatters: Hibernate 6 work often overlaps with Jakarta namespace migration in Spring Boot 3 and modern Jakarta EE stacks.
risks.recommendation: Track namespace migration separately so ORM API risk is not hidden behind import rewrites.

validationNotes: Catalog-backed reports prove the report shape and known risk vocabulary, not checkout compile success.
Checkout-backed validation should capture compile and test status before a migration estimate is treated as execution-ready.
A passing compile/test run increases confidence, but domain persistence behavior still needs project-specific test coverage.
Failed validation evidence is still useful when it honestly records build compatibility, runtime, or dependency problems.

benchmarks.name: Hibernate ORM Demos
benchmarks.note: Small Hibernate-focused examples that show direct ORM API usage.

benchmarks.name: Hibernate Test Case Templates
benchmarks.note: Templates that exercise Hibernate-specific testing and reproduction patterns.

benchmarks.name: Hypersistence Utils
benchmarks.note: Custom type and advanced Hibernate integration examples.

benchmarks.name: High Performance Java Persistence
benchmarks.note: Persistence examples with direct Hibernate and JPA usage patterns.

benchmarks.name: Baeldung Hibernate Tutorials
benchmarks.note: Tutorial material with broad Hibernate API and XML mapping examples.

limits: The readiness report does not prove runtime query correctness.
The readiness report does not replace schema migration review.
The readiness report does not guarantee custom Hibernate types are compatible with the target version.
The readiness report does not apply source transformations for Hibernate APIs.
